items.prompt_md: Why does the reversed traversal move to a neighbor when `heights[neighbor] >= heights[current]`, instead of `<=`?
options: Because the traversal walks backward — from the ocean uphill — so it follows the reverse of the forward "flow downhill" rule
Because higher cells are always closer to the ocean
It's a typo in the reference solution; it should be `<=`
Because the grid is guaranteed to be sorted by height
items.answer: Because the traversal walks backward — from the ocean uphill — so it follows the reverse of the forward "flow downhill" rule
items.explanation_md: Water flows from X to Y forward when `heights[Y] <= heights[X]`. Walking that edge in reverse, from Y to X, means X must satisfy `heights[X] >= heights[Y]` — uphill from the ocean's perspective.

items.prompt_md: You must find every cell in a grid that can reach BOTH of two opposite grid edges, following a cheap forward flow rule, without checking each cell individually against both edges. Which approach fits?
options: Run a boundary-seeded reverse traversal from each edge separately, then intersect the two reachable sets
Sort all cells by height and process them from lowest to highest
Union-Find every cell with its lower neighbors and count components
Binary search on the height threshold
items.answer: Run a boundary-seeded reverse traversal from each edge separately, then intersect the two reachable sets
items.explanation_md: "Reach multiple targets, forward check is cheap but running it from every cell is quadratic" is the boundary-reverse-traversal fingerprint: flip the direction, flood from each target once, and combine the reachable sets.

items.prompt_md: For an R × C grid, what is the total time complexity of the two boundary-seeded reverse traversals plus the intersection?
items.explanation_md: Each reverse traversal visits every cell at most once — O(R × C) — and intersecting two sets of at most R × C cells is also O(R × C), so the whole algorithm stays linear in the grid size.
